1: The parable of the dishonest manager (oikonomos — steward) is addressed 'to the disciples' (Luke 16:1) rather than the Pharisees, making it an inner-circle teaching about kingdom economics. The manager who is 'wasting the master's possessions' (diaskorpizōn ta hyparchonta autou) uses the same verb as the prodigal who 'squandered his property' (Luke 15:13 — dieskorpisen). The connection is deliberate: the dishonest manager and the prodigal both misuse entrusted resources — but the dishonest manager responds to his crisis more shrewdly than many do to the kingdom's crisis.
2: 'What is this that I hear about you? Turn in the account of your management, for you can no longer be manager' — the manager's accountability before the master is the eschatological accounting that awaits every steward. Christ is the Master who will call all stewards to account: 'each of us will give an account of himself to God' (Romans 14:12). The dismissal-notice creates urgency — the same urgency the gospel creates: the time of stewardship is not indefinite. The crisis of being called to account is the moment that reveals true priorities.
3: 'What shall I do, since my master is taking the management away from me? I am not strong enough to dig, and I am ashamed to beg' — the manager's self-assessment (not strong enough, too ashamed) is honest about his limitations in a way that leads to creative action. His cunning is not the virtue commended but its application — using the present moment of crisis to secure the future. Christ calls disciples to the same crisis-awareness: the present moment is the moment of decision; the future is shaped by present action. The manager's cunning is redirected in Luke 16:9 toward kingdom investment.
4: 'I have decided what to do, so that when I am removed from management, people may receive me into their houses' — the manager's strategy (using present resources to secure future welcome) is the principle Christ commends: use 'unrighteous mammon' (Luke 16:9) to make friends who will receive you into the 'eternal tents' (eternal dwellings). The kingdom application: wealth is unrighteous (it cannot secure eternity), but it can be used to secure kingdom-relationships that will endure. Generosity now creates a welcome later — the economics of the kingdom inverted from the world's economy.
5: 'So, summoning his master's debtors one by one, he said to the first, How much do you owe my master? He said, A hundred measures of oil' — the manager's reduction of debts on the master's accounts is the commended shrewdness. The application point (Luke 16:9) is not that dishonesty is commended but that urgent, creative, future-securing action with present resources is. Christ himself reduces the debt of sinners — 'your sins are forgiven you' (Luke 7:48) — not through dishonesty but through his own payment of the debt on the cross. He who paid what was owed secures for sinners eternal welcome.
6: 'He said, Take your bill, and sit down quickly and write fifty' — the rapid reduction (from 100 to 50 measures of oil) creates immediate obligation and gratitude in the debtors. The manager's swift action in the time of crisis secures his future. The application to discipleship is the urgency of present action for eternal investment. The 'quickly' (tacheōs) of the manager's instruction is the urgency of the gospel: now is the acceptable time, now is the day of salvation (2 Corinthians 6:2). Delayed response to the kingdom's crisis is the failure the parable warns against.
7: 'Then he said to another, And how much do you owe? He said, A hundred measures of wheat. He said to him, Take your bill and write eighty' — the second debt-reduction continues the manager's systematic strategy. The wheat-oil symbolism may be significant: wheat and oil are the basic sustenance goods of Palestinian agriculture — the master's wealth is in the basics of life that feed people. The reduction of food-debts creates bonds of practical obligation. Christ's kingdom economics similarly reduces the heaviest burdens and creates bonds of gratitude-obligation (Luke 6:38 — 'the measure you give will be the measure you get back').
8: 'The master commended the dishonest manager for his shrewdness. For the sons of this age are more shrewd in dealing with their own generation than the sons of light' — the master's commendation is specifically for shrewdness (phronimōs — practically wise) not for dishonesty. Christ applies this: 'the sons of this age' (worldly people) apply more practical wisdom to their temporal affairs than 'the sons of light' (disciples) apply to eternal affairs. The diagnosis is pointed: Christians often treat eternal investment with less urgency and creativity than businesspeople treat financial investment. The parable calls for parity of urgency.
9: 'And I tell you, make friends for yourselves by means of unrighteous wealth, so that when it fails they may receive you into the eternal dwellings' — the kingdom application: use money (unrighteous mammon — mamōnas adikias) generously to make kingdom-friends (through charity, hospitality, generosity) who will be waiting in the eternal dwellings when money fails (at death). The 'eternal tents' (aiōnious skēnas) echoes the Feast of Tabernacles imagery and foreshadows the new creation's dwelling-places (John 14:2 — 'many rooms in my Father's house'). Generosity is not merely a virtue but an eternal investment strategy.
10: 'One who is faithful in a very little is also faithful in much, and one who is dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much' — the faithfulness-principle makes wealth-stewardship a training-ground for kingdom stewardship. The 'very little' of earthly wealth trains the character that will be trusted with 'much' in the kingdom (Luke 19:17 — 'because you have been faithful in a very little, you shall have authority over ten cities'). Christ himself was 'faithful over God's house as a son' (Hebrews 3:6) — the model of the one entrusted with everything because he was faithful in everything.
11: 'If then you have not been faithful in the unrighteous wealth, who will entrust to you the true riches?' — the 'true riches' (alethinon) in contrast to 'unrighteous wealth' (mamōnas adikias) is the kingdom's genuine treasure: eternal life, relationship with God, the inheritance of the new creation. Faithfulness with money is the prerequisite for being trusted with true riches — not because wealth is important but because how we handle the relatively unimportant reveals the character that will handle the truly important. The eschatological judgment includes an accounting of earthly stewardship.
12: 'And if you have not been faithful in that which is another's, who will give you that which is your own?' — the earthly wealth that disciples manage is 'another's' (allotriō — someone else's, namely God's). Everything belongs to God; disciples are stewards, not owners. Faithfulness in stewarding God's resources (treating them as the owner's, not as one's own) qualifies for receiving what is 'your own' — the inheritance that Christ secures for believers (Ephesians 1:13-14 — the Spirit as the guarantee of our inheritance). The faithful steward becomes the heir.
13: 'No servant can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and money' — the impossibility of serving both God and mammon is not merely a practical difficulty but a structural impossibility: lordship is exclusive. Christ is Lord (Kyrios — the same word as God/YHWH throughout the LXX); mammon cannot share the lordship-role. The Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4 — 'The LORD our God, the LORD is one') demands exclusive devotion. Christ who is himself the Lord calls disciples to the absolute monotheism of kingdom-loyalty.
14: 'The Pharisees, who were lovers of money, heard all these things, and they ridiculed him' — the Pharisees' love of money (philargyroi — lovers of money/silver) and their ridicule (exemyktērizon — turned up their noses, sneered) is the response of those who have divided their service between God and money and resent the exposure. Their religiosity and their financial ambition coexist — a coexistence Christ's teaching makes impossible. The Pharisees who 'justify themselves before men' (Luke 16:15) use religious performance to maintain social standing while practicing the divided service Christ has just condemned.
15: 'And he said to them, You are those who justify yourselves before men, but God knows your hearts. For what is exalted among men is an abomination before God' — the divine heart-knowledge (God knows your hearts — kardiognōstēs, used of God in Acts 1:24 and Luke 15:8) cuts through the social performance of righteousness. The Pharisees are justified before men (their social reputation is excellent) but their hearts are known to God. The 'abomination' (bdelygma — the strongest word of divine rejection in the LXX, used for idols) of human social-status-seeking before God reveals that self-justification is a form of idolatry: making oneself the god of one's social world.
16: 'The Law and the Prophets were until John; since then the good news of the kingdom of God is preached, and everyone forces his way into it' — the historical turning-point: John marks the end of the Law-and-Prophets era; Christ inaugurates the kingdom-proclamation era. The 'forcing one's way in' (biazetai — either 'the kingdom presses forward forcefully' or 'everyone presses forcefully into it') suggests the urgent, overwhelming energy of the kingdom's arrival. Christ himself is the one 'who is to come' (Luke 7:19-20) at whose arrival the old order transitions to the new.
17: 'But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one dot of the Law to become void' — the continuity of the Law within the new kingdom-era: Christ fulfills rather than abolishes (Matthew 5:17). The Law's requirements are not reduced in the kingdom; they are fulfilled in Christ (Romans 8:4 — 'the righteous requirement of the law might be fulfilled in us, who walk not according to the flesh but according to the Spirit'). The 'dot' (keraia — the smallest stroke of a Hebrew letter) is secured in Christ's perfect obedience and atonement.
18: 'Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery, and he who marries a woman divorced from her husband commits adultery' — the divorce saying in this context addresses the Pharisees' use of legal loopholes to circumvent the covenant-faithfulness the Law requires. Christ restores the original creation-intent of marriage (Genesis 2:24 — 'one flesh'). He who is himself the faithful bridegroom (Ephesians 5:25-32 — 'as Christ loved the church and gave himself for her') defines the standard of covenant fidelity. His own faithfulness to his people unto death is the model for human covenant fidelity.
19: 'There was a rich man who was clothed in purple and fine linen and who feasted sumptuously every day' — the rich man's clothing (purple — the most expensive dye; fine linen — priestly/royal cloth) and daily feasting marks him as among the wealthiest of the wealthy. He is not described as evil or wicked — only as wealthy and oblivious. His sin is not active cruelty but radical disconnection from the suffering at his gate. Christ who 'had no place to lay his head' (Luke 9:58) and whose mother offered the poverty offering (Luke 2:24) stands as the implicit contrast: the one who became poor so that we might become rich (2 Corinthians 8:9).
20: 'And at his gate was laid a poor man named Lazarus, covered with sores' — Lazarus (the only named character in any of Jesus's parables — a detail that has been much discussed) bears a name meaning 'God has helped' or 'God will help.' His condition (covered with sores, at the gate, longing for crumbs) is the maximum degradation of the poor. He lies at the gate — on the threshold of the rich man's abundance — and receives nothing. The gate that separates them in life becomes the 'great chasm' (Luke 16:26) that separates them in death. Christ crosses every such gate and chasm: he goes to Lazarus-the-poor (Luke 4:18; Luke 7:22) and raises Lazarus-the-dead (John 11).
21: 'Who desired to be fed with what fell from the rich man's table. Moreover, even the dogs came and licked his sores' — Lazarus's passive reception (dogs lick his sores — an act of kindness from animals that he could not resist or welcome) is the depth of his helplessness. The 'crumbs from the table' language echoes the Syrophoenician woman's faith-request (Matthew 15:27 — 'even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their master's table'). But Lazarus receives not even crumbs. The rich man is the 'table' that should be feeding others; he withholds what Lazarus needs. Christ is the table that never withholds (John 6:35 — 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger').
22: 'The poor man died and was carried by the angels to Abraham's side. The rich man also died and was buried' — the two deaths and their contrasts: Lazarus is 'carried by angels' to Abraham; the rich man 'was buried' with the ceremony his wealth demands. In death, the reversal begins: the one with no burial worth noting is transported by angels; the one with a proper burial goes to Hades. The angels who serve Christ (Luke 22:43 — 'an angel from heaven appeared to him, strengthening him') also serve the Lazaruses of the world. Christ himself was buried — like the rich man — but rose, unlike either.
23: 'And in Hades, being in torment, he lifted up his eyes and saw Abraham far off and Lazarus at his side' — the rich man's lifting of his eyes in Hades to see Abraham and Lazarus 'far off' reverses the life-situation: in life, Lazarus was at the gate (near but not received); in death, the rich man is far from Abraham (distant from the covenant). The covenant promise (to be with Abraham) is fulfilled in Lazarus; the covenant failure (of ignoring the needy at one's gate) is exposed in the rich man. Christ who is himself 'Abraham's son' (in the sense of the covenant's fulfillment) grants Abraham's bosom to those who receive his kingdom.
24: 'Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus to dip the end of his finger in water and cool my tongue, for I am in anguish in this flame' — the rich man still addresses Lazarus as an errand-runner ('send Lazarus') — the same posture as in life, where he would have directed servants to bring him things. He has not understood even in death that Lazarus is his equal or superior. His request for a drop of water echoes Lazarus's longing for crumbs — both longing for what is denied. The one who denied crumbs now asks for drops; the economy of mercy he refused operates against him.
25: 'Child, remember that you in your lifetime received your good things, and Lazarus in like manner bad things; but now he is comforted here, and you are in anguish' — Abraham's address 'child' (teknon) to the rich man maintains familial relationship even in judgment — he is addressed with the same warmth as the father's address to the elder son (Luke 15:31). But the reversal is irrevocable: good-things-in-life → anguish-in-death; bad-things-in-life → comfort-in-death. The Magnificat (Luke 1:52-53) has reached its eschatological fulfillment: 'he has filled the hungry with good things, and the rich he has sent away empty.'
26: 'And besides all this, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed, in order that those who would pass from here to you may not be able, and none may cross from there to us' — the great chasm (chasma mega) is the irreversibility of the post-death state. The parable does not teach annihilation or purgatory but the permanent fixing of the eternal state after death. The cross is the place where Christ bridges the chasm — not between hell and heaven but between the Father's presence and the far country of sin. He who descended into the far country (Incarnation) and into the depths (death) and rose again bridges the only chasm that grace can cross.
27: 'Then I beg you, father, to send him to my father's house — for I have five brothers — so that he may warn them, lest they also come into this place of torment' — the rich man's concern for his five brothers reveals that his conscience knows what they need: warning, repentance. The irony is that the warning they need is available: 'they have Moses and the Prophets' (Luke 16:29). The five brothers already have the Scriptures that point to Christ. The problem is not lack of information but the will to receive it. The rich man who ignored Scripture in life asks for a resurrection-miracle for his brothers — but Abraham's response points back to the same Scripture.
28: 'I have five brothers, so that he may warn them, lest they also come into this place of torment' — the concern for the five brothers (five = the complete number of siblings in the family) is the only act of love the rich man performs in the parable. But it is too late for him and unnecessary for them: they have what is needed. The parable implicitly indicts the assumption that miraculous signs would produce faith. Christ himself will raise Lazarus (John 11) and many 'will not believe' (John 11:45-48 — the chief priests plan to kill both Jesus and Lazarus). Signs do not produce faith where the heart is hardened.
29: 'But Abraham said, They have Moses and the Prophets; let them hear them' — Moses and the Prophets (the Law and the Prophets) are sufficient witnesses to the truth about wealth, poverty, judgment, and the coming Messiah. Christ says elsewhere that the Scriptures 'bear witness about me' (John 5:39-40). The five brothers who have Moses and the Prophets have the witness of Christ in the OT's testimony. The rich man's request for a resurrection-visitor is the request for extraordinary evidence when ordinary evidence (Scripture) is refused. 'If they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be convinced if someone rises from the dead' (Luke 16:31).
30: 'No, father Abraham, but if someone goes to them from the dead, they will repent' — the rich man's insistence that a resurrection would produce repentance is the assumption that extraordinary evidence overcomes resistance. The assumption is refuted by history: Christ rose from the dead, and the same chief priests who knew about the resurrection (Matthew 28:11-15 — they bribed the guards to spread a false story) did not repent. The resurrection was and is the decisive evidence; those who refuse it do not lack evidence but willingness. 'He has risen from the dead' (Matthew 28:7) is available to all; repentance requires not more evidence but a new heart.
31: 'He said to him, If they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be convinced if someone rises from the dead' — the final word of the parable is the word Christ speaks to his own resurrection's reception: the Scriptures are sufficient witness; those who refuse them will not be persuaded by the resurrection either. This was demonstrated in Jerusalem: 'many of the Jews who had come to Mary and had seen what he did believed in him, but some of them went to the Pharisees' (John 11:45-46). The resurrection of Lazarus produced both faith and intensified opposition. The resurrection of Christ did the same. The heart that refuses Moses will refuse the risen Christ.
